name: Judith Butler
practice: Performativity theory, vulnerability as political category, precarious life
approach: The body is not a given — it is performed, regulated, made legible through repetition. Vulnerability is not weakness — it is the fundamental condition of embodied existence. A politics built on invulnerability is a politics built on denial.
body_of_work: Gender Trouble (1990), Bodies That Matter (1993), Precarious Life (2004), Notes Toward a Performative Theory of Assembly (2015). Transformed how we think about bodies, identity, and public space.
why_instructive: The dome's layers perform the person into legibility — each layer is a way of reading the body. Butler warns us: the categories we use to help can also constrain. The dome must hold its own categories lightly.

dome_relevance.1: Legal — how law performs and constrains identity categories
dome_relevance.4: Health — how medical categories produce the bodies they claim to describe
dome_relevance.11: Creativity — identity as creative practice, not fixed attribute

sphere_relevance.5: Social — public assembly as political body, vulnerability in shared space
sphere_relevance.7: Activation — gathering as performative act, bodies in space
sphere_relevance.9: Policy — how policy categories produce the populations they govern
